fr:mainmatter.html:p: The kernel-side data structure holding all per-process state. There is one PCB per Process, threaded together into the process list. The PCB is what the OS reads and writes during a Context switch: the saved register set lives here, as does the process state, the open files, the parent pointer, and so on.
An example from xv6:
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. Threads need an analogous thread control block; see Concurrency Intro.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The 15 most recently modified trees, by git commit time.
User-defined type in C++ that is a set of named values designed to make code more readable and less error prone.

html:pre.html:code:
enum class Color { red, green, blue };
Color c = Color::red;

fr:mainmatter.html:p: If you want enumerator values to be int, you can use enum.
fr:mainmatter.html:div:
Example. enum Color { red, green, blue };
int color = green // color = 1 Note that this is an error if Color is enum class.
Remark. Use enumerations to represent sets of named constants
Remark. Prefer enum class to enum when possible

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Replaces the calling Process's program with a new executable. exec() does not return on success — the original code, data, stack, and heap are discarded and the new program runs in their place. The PID is unchanged.
What exec() does internally:

html:ol.html:li: Loads the new executable's code and static data, overwriting the current.
Re-initializes stack and heap.
Jumps to the new program's entry point with arguments passed as argv.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The canonical Unix pattern is fork() then exec() — the shell uses this to launch a command without becoming that command:
Redirection is implemented by opening a file in the child between fork() and exec(), attached to STDOUT_FILENO:
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. The many variants — execl, execle, execlp, execv, execvp, execvP — differ in how they take arguments (list vs. vector), whether they use the PATH environment variable, and whether they take an explicit environment.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The Unix system call that duplicates the calling Process. After fork() returns, two processes exist where there was one. The child gets its own copy of:

html:ol.html:li: Address space.
General-purpose registers.
Program counter — the child returns from fork() at the same instruction the parent does.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The two processes are distinguished by fork()'s return value:
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. Which process is scheduled first after fork() is non-deterministic. Use wait() if you need ordering.
Remark. The copy is logical, not physical: real implementations use Copy-on-write so the duplicated address space only allocates fresh pages when one side writes to them.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The kernel-side data structure holding all per-process state. There is one PCB per Process, threaded together into the process list. The PCB is what the OS reads and writes during a Context switch: the saved register set lives here, as does the process state, the open files, the parent pointer, and so on.
An example from xv6:
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. Threads need an analogous thread control block; see Concurrency Intro.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Each class with a virtual function gets a virtual table: a static, per-class, array of function pointers with one entry per virtual function.
The virtual table is created by the compiler and is stored in the object's memory.
fr:mainmatter.html:pre: vtable layout: [ offset-to-top ] // slot -2 [ type_info* ] // slot -1 ← RTTI [ &virtual_fn_0 ] // slot 0 [ &virtual_fn_1 ] // slot 1 ...
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. The type_info pointer is needed to support runtime type-checking / dynamic casting .
fr:mainmatter.html:p: During construction, the vptr is initialized to point to the base class's vtable. In each classes vtable, there is a thunk (offset-to-top) that tells

html:div.html:pre: offset 0: [ vptr_A ] ← D's primary vtable (A subobject) offset 8: [ a ] offset 16: [ vptr_B ] ← D's secondary vtable (B subobject) offset 24: [ b ] offset 32: [ d ]

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Every object of a class with a virtual function carries a hidden vptr (virtual pointer) that points to the class's virtual table.
Each class with a virtual function gets a virtual table: a static, per-class, array of function pointers with one entry per virtual function.
The virtual table is created by the compiler and is stored in the object's memory.
fr:mainmatter.html:pre: vtable layout: [ offset-to-top ] // slot -2 [ type_info* ] // slot -1 ← RTTI [ &virtual_fn_0 ] // slot 0 [ &virtual_fn_1 ] // slot 1 ...
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. The type_info pointer is needed to support runtime type-checking / dynamic casting .
fr:mainmatter.html:p: During construction, the vptr is initialized to point to the base class's vtable. In each classes vtable, there is a thunk (offset-to-top) that tells

html:div.html:pre: offset 0: [ vptr_A ] ← D's primary vtable (A subobject) offset 8: [ a ] offset 16: [ vptr_B ] ← D's secondary vtable (B subobject) offset 24: [ b ] offset 32: [ d ]

fr:mainmatter.html:p: An abstract type is a type that completely insulates a user from implementation details.
Since we do not know anything about the representation (in particular, the size) we must allocate everything on heap.
A union is a struct in which all members are allocated at the same address. This ensures that the union occupies only as much space as its largest member.
A union can hold a value for only one member at a time.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Problem. No automatic lifetime management for non-trivial types. If a union holds an object with a non-trivial constructor/destructor (like std::string), the compiler won't call them for you. You must manually placement-new and explicitly call the destructor, and getting this wrong causes leaks or corruption. union U { std::string s; int i; }; // destructor not called automatically

fr:mainmatter.html:p: This covers the math behind the online softmax algorithm required by Flash Attention (v1).
The first thing we must consider is numerical precision. Namely, the original softmax operation is given by
However, when operating with low precision data types (i.e. float16) the maximum value is 65536. Therefore, with \exp we can easily get numerically overflow.

To get around this, the online softmax algorithm adheres to the safe softmax. For safe softmax, we simply multiply the numerator and denominator by \exp (-m), where m = \max \{x_{1}, \ldots , x_n\}. This gives
Since x_i - m \leq 0, this ensures that each \exp (x_i - m) is bounded in a safe range.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Problem. Note that computing the \max over \mathbf {x} means that we must have all of the logits in a row of QK^T materialized in SMEM, or first loop over all of them to compute the max before computing the normalization constant.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The online softmax algorithm fuses the max reduction into the normalization pass, so a single sweep over the row suffices to compute the normalization with the max. The key difficulty is that the denominator depends on n for each i i.e.

Therefore, we must find a recursive relationship such that d_{i+1} = f(d_i, m_i, x_{i+1}) for some function f. This would then allow us to locally compute d_{i+1} with only the local information of current x_{i+1} and the two running statistics d_i and m_i. This is exactly the kind of algorithm that is conducive to memory tiling.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Question. What is f(d_i, m_i, x_{i+1})?
Answer. Consider d_{i+1} = d_i \cdot \exp (m_{i} - m_{i + 1}) + \exp (x_{i + 1} - m_{i + 1}).

Proof. This can be seen by directly rewriting d_{i + 1}.
\begin {align*} d_{i + 1} &= \sum _{j = 1}^{i+1} \exp (x_j - m_{i + 1}) \\ &= \left (\sum _{j = 1}^{i} \exp (x_j - m_{i+1})\right ) + \exp (x_{i+1} - m_{i+1}) \\ &= \left (\sum _{j = 1}^{i} \exp (x_j - m_{i})\right )\exp (m_{i} - m_{i + 1}) + \exp (x_{i+1} - m_{i+1}) \\ &= d_{i} \exp (m_{i} - m_{i + 1}) + \exp (x_{i+1} - m_{i+1}) \end {align*}
fr:mainmatter.html:p: The running denominator d is rescaled by e^{m - m_{\text {new}}} each time a new maximum is found, keeping it consistent with the safe-softmax shift.

html:pre.html:code: m = float("-inf") # running max of the logits d = 0.0 # running softmax denominator for i in range(n): # one fused sweep for the stats m_new = max(m, x[i]) d = d * exp(m - m_new) + exp(x[i] - m_new) m = m_new p = [exp(x[i] - m) / d for i in range(n)] # second pass: normalized probs

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. Unfortunately, we cannot get around the second pass to compute the normalized output for \mathbf {p}. However, this is not the case in Flash Attention (v1).

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Unlike in the online softmax, we are not interested in the outputted probability vector \mathbf {p}. Instead we are interested in the entire output matrix O given by
For more details see self-attention.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Notation. In correspondence with the notation in the online softmax, we define the following: x_{i,j} = \mathbf {q}_i \cdot \mathbf {k}_j, where \mathbf {q}_i is the ith row of Q and \mathbf {k}_j is the jth row of K. Similarly, \mathbf {o}_i is the ith row of the output matrix O given by \mathbf {o}_i = \sum _{j = 1}^{n} \frac {\exp (x_{i,j} - m_n)}{d_n} \cdot \mathbf {v}_j
fr:mainmatter.html:p: Now following the template provided by the online softmax, we must find a recurrence for \mathbf {o}_i. Namely, we will consider the sequence \mathbf {o}_{i,k}' given by \mathbf {o}_{i,k}' = \sum _{j = 1}^{k} \frac {\exp (x_{i,j} - m_k)}{d_k} \cdot \mathbf {v}_j, such that \mathbf {o}_{i,n}' = \mathbf {o}_n
fr:mainmatter.html:div:
Claim. We have the following recurrence for \mathbf {o}_{i,k}':
\mathbf {o}_{i,k}' = \mathbf {o}_{i, k-1} \cdot \frac {d_{k - 1} \cdot \exp (m_{k-1} - m_{k})}{d_k} + \frac {\exp (x_{i,j} - m_k)}{d_k} \cdot \mathbf {v}_k

Proof. We will proceed by directly rewriting the recurrence for \mathbf {o}_{i,k}' as
\begin {align*} \mathbf {o}_{i,k}' &= \sum _{j = 1}^{k} \frac {\exp (x_{i,j} - m_k)}{d_k} \cdot \mathbf {v}_j \\ &= \left (\sum _{j = 1}^{k - 1} \frac {\exp (x_{i,j} - m_{k-1})}{d_{k-1}} \cdot \mathbf {v}_j\right ) \frac {d_{k - 1} \cdot \exp (m_{k-1} - m_{k})}{d_k} + \frac {\exp (x_{i,k} - m_k)}{d_k} \cdot \mathbf {v}_k \\ &= \mathbf {o}_{i, k-1} \cdot \frac {d_{k - 1} \cdot \exp (m_{k-1} - m_{k})}{d_k} + \frac {\exp (x_{i,k} - m_k)}{d_k} \cdot \mathbf {v}_k .\end {align*}
fr:mainmatter.html:p: Thus, we are left with the following algorithm

html:pre.html:code: for i in range(N): # one query row at a time m = float("-inf") # running max of the logits d = 0.0 # running softmax denominator o = zeros(head_dim) # running output accumulator for j in range(M): # stream over keys/values x = q[i] @ k[j] # logit x_{i,j} m_new = max(m, x) d_new = d * exp(m - m_new) + exp(x - m_new) o = o * (d * exp(m - m_new) / d_new) + (exp(x - m_new) / d_new) * v[j] m, d = m_new, d_new out[i] = o

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. A key benefit of this algorithm is that it is entirely parallelizable across rows. This means that we can loop across entire tiles like in the GEMM case.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Formal notebook for my journey in Stanford's CS336 as part of my LLM self-study endeavors.

Trivially, we can do character-based tokenization i.e. mapping each character to a unique number. For example, \mathsf {chr}(97) = \text {``a''} and \mathsf {ord}(\text {``a''}) = 97. The compression ratio will be > 1 because each token corresponds to a character potentially consisting of more than one byte.

html:ol.html:li: Very large vocabulary
Many characters are quite rare.

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. There is a fundamental tradeoff between compression ratio and vocabulary size. As an example, suppose we map every sequence of n bytes to a token. This would achieve a compression ratio of n. However, this would require 256^n tokens in our vocabulary.
fr:mainmatter.html:p: Problem 2 was not entirely straight-forward to me. This actually reminds me of an idea from my information theory course, where we can assume we are given a "prior" over human language then we want to map low-probability characters to large strings in order to minimize the expected length (see huffman coding).

In byte-based tokenization, each byte is a token achieving a compression ratio of exactly 1, where a byte \mapsto [0, 256] . This is very elegant in the sense that our vocabulary size is fixed at 256.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Problem. However, with this, the length of our encoding is the number of bytes in the string, which will become too long.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: In word-based tokenization, we simply split the string into the corresponding words then map each of these to a unique integer.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Problem. The vocabulary size is "unbounded" i.e. if we see a word we have not seen before then we need to extend the vocabulary size.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: For the byte pair encoding (BPE), we instead train the tokenizer on raw text to automatically determine the vocabulary.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. In GPT2, they first split the raw text into words and then do this byte pair encoding.

Remark. There are a few additional implementation details to consider for project 1.
\mathsf {encode} loops over all merges. Only loop over merges that matter.
Detect and preserve special tokens (e.g. <|endoftext|>)
Use pre-tokenization (GPT2 regex)

fr:mainmatter.html:p: We start by discussing a few essential questions one hopes to answer when they set out to train a large model.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Question. How long to train a 70b parameter model on 15T tokens on 1024 H100s?

Answer. Mysteriously, we spawn from the ether (will be explained later) that total_flops = 6 * 70e9 * 15e12. Intuitively, we know that we need to perform some computation for each parameter for each token. However, the 6 is still an ominous mystery. Given the total flops, we then can just take the GPUs FLOP/s multiply by the number of seconds in the day and divide total_flops by this number i.e. days = total_flops / flops_per_day

Remark. In practice, we must also consider the model flop utilization (MFU). Basically, our algorithm cannot utilize the entirety of the maximum FLOP/s provided by the hardware due to things like memory bandwidth, etc. To account for this in the calculation, they just take flops_per_day / 2.
Question. What is the largest model that you can train on 8 H100s using AdamW?

Answer. An H100 has 80gb of shared memory i.e. h100_bytes = 80e9. The calculation then proceeds as # parameters, gradients, optimizer state bytest_per_param = 4 + 4 + (4 + 4) num_parameters = (h100_bytes * 8) / bytes_per_parameter what we need each of parameters, gradients, optimizer state will become clear later.
Remark. In practice, we also need to account for activations, which will depend on both batch size and sequence length.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Tensor Memory. Tensors are stored as a floating point number. Memory is determined by (i) number of values in tensor and (ii) data type of each value.

float16: here, we decrease both the fraction and exponent bits. Importantly, there are only 5 exponent bits making the effective range (2^{-6}, 2^{6}). The dynamic range for small numbers is pretty bad. This can cause small numbers to "die" effectively killing these neurons for the remainder of learning due to backpropagation.
bfloat16: to fix the problems with float16, they instead keep 8 exponent bits and reduce the fraction bits to 7. This maintains the same dynamic range as float32 while still only using 16 bits.

fp8: Two variants
E4M3: has 4 exponent bits and 3 mantissa bits
E5M2: has 5 exponent bits and 2 mantissa bits Requires modern Hopper Architecture i.e. H100 to perform operations. Also supported by TPUs.

html:ol.html:li: float32 training works but requires lots of memory
Using lower precision data types allows you to train larger models due to lower memory requirements, but can cause instability
To optimize this tradeoff, use mixed-precision training.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: In pytorch, tensors are contiguous blocks of memory, where their shape is controlled by strides. A stride basically just refers to how far you have to jump in physical memory to correspond to incrementing the logical index i.e. in a (2, 3) matrix each row requires jumping 3 spots in physical memory. Thus, stride[1] = 3.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. Calling .view((m, n)) just changes the stride but does not modify the physical memory. If you call .view that results in a non-contiguous view, then you cannot call .view again because it assumes contiguous indexing to properly adjust the strides. To deal with this, they have .contiguous() and .reshape() is basically .contiguous().view().
Remark. All elementwise operations make a copy i.e. x + y. A useful function for masked attention is triu().

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter:
Einops is a useful python library to keep track of high-dimensional matrix operations i.e. matmuls over batch_size, seqlen, etc. High level overview
Initialize like X[float.tensor, "batch seq hidden"] = torch.tensor((2, 3, 4))
Can perform matmul with named dims like z = einsum(x, y, "batch seq1 hidden, batch seq2 hidden -> batch seq1 seq2")
reduce: x = reduce(x, "x y z -> x y 1") is just like mean(x, axis=-1)
rearrange: x = rearrange(x, "... (heads hidden1) -> ... heads hidden 1")

fr:mainmatter.html:p: We refer to the total number of floating point operatings as FLOPS. Alternatively, we refer to the number of floating point operations per second as FLOP/s.
Given a promised FLOP/s, the model flop utilization is given by \frac {\text {actual FLOP/s}}{\text {promised FLOP/s}}

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Example. Performing a matrix multiplication A \cdot B for A \in \mathbb {R}^{(B \times D)} and B \in \mathbb {R}^{(D \times K)}, requires 2 \times B \times D \times K FLOPS

fr:mainmatter.html:p: I hope to do a formal blog touring optimizers because I have written a paper on deep learning optimizers for my optimization course. Due to this, I will leave this blank for now. A brief list of the covered concepts are below:

html:ul.html:li: momentum
adagrad
RMSProp
Adam

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A key formula to remember: \text {total memory} \approx 4 \times (\text {params} + \text {activations} + \text {grad size} + \text {optimizer state size})

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. Another useful trick is to use low precision for forward pass activations, then use float32 for gradients and network parameters.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Pre-norm vs. Post-norm. The first architecture variation discussed is when to apply the layer norm. As can be seen in the figure below, rather than apply the layer norm in the residual stream. They instead place it before the FFN and MHA layers.
They actually found that adding layer normalization before and after the MHA and FFN works the best. This is known as double norm.
RMSNorm vs. LayerNorm. Another useful trick is to use the RMSNorm instead of LayerNorm.

The RMS norm is simply the layer norm without the added bias term and centering i.e. \mathrm {RMSNorm}: \mathbb {R}^d \to \mathbb {R}^d, \quad \mathbf {x} \mapsto \frac {\mathbf {x}}{\sqrt {\mathrm {Var}[\mathbf {x}_i] + \epsilon }}*\gamma . This is used to spare memory movement of layernorm, where the bias term \beta \in \mathbb {R}^d and has been found to be empirically almost if not as good.
One might wonder if matrix multiplies are the only thing that matters what can such a small change really accomplish.

html:ul.html:li: Due to memory movement, despite being .17% of FLOPS, provided 25% speedup in runtime!!

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. Most people apparently just drop the bias term and keep the centering i.e. subtracting the mean. This makes sense because computing the mean does not require loading any additional information back to memory.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Despite a long list of activations, the two focused on are ReLU and GELU.

The rectified linear unit(ReLU) is defined as \mathrm {ReLU} : \mathbb {R}^d \to \mathbb {R}^d, \quad \mathbf {x} \mapsto \max (0, \mathbf {x}), where the \max is done elementwise i.e. \mathrm {ReLU}(\mathbf {x})_i = \max (0, x_i) This provides "nice" gradients, making it common when training neural networks.

The Gaussian Error Linear Unit (GELU) is a slight modification to the ReLU to account for the non-differentiability at 0. Namely, \mathrm {GELU}(\mathbf {x}) : \mathbb {R}^d \to \mathbb {R}^d, \quad \mathbf {x} \mapsto \mathbf {x} \cdot \psi (\mathbf {x}), where \psi (\mathbf {x}) = \mathrm {CDF}(\mathcal {N}(\mathbf {x}, \mathbf {I}))

html:figure.html:figcaption: GELU vs. ReLU activation and derivative. Taken from here.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The Swish activation is given by \mathrm {Swish}(\mathbf {x}) : \mathbb {R}^d \to \mathbb {R}^d, \quad \mathbf {x} \mapsto \mathbf {x} s(\mathbf {x}), where s : \mathbb {R}^d \to \mathbb {R}^d is the sigmoid function.

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. The extra computation required by GELU or Swish is a non-factor because FLOPS are dominated by matrix multiplication and there is no increase in memory pressure.

fr:mainmatter.html:p:
A gated linear unit is an activation function combined with an elementwise multiplication i.e.
\mathrm {ReGLU} = \mathrm {ReLU}(\mathbf {x}) \otimes \mathbf {V} \mathbf {x},
\mathrm {SwiGLU} = \mathrm {Swish}(\mathbf {x}) \otimes \mathbf {V} \mathbf {x}, where \mathrm {ReLU} is the ReLU activation and \mathrm {Swish} is the Swish activation respectively.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. The matrix \mathbf {V} introduces additional learnable parameters. Therefore, an architecture that uses gating typically reduces their other parameters by a factor of \frac {2}{3}.

fr:title.text: Computing FFN and MHA in parallel is free speedup

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Computing FFN and MHA in parallel is free speedup

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Another small trick is to parallelize computation. Traditionally, one computes the \mathrm {MHA} output and then passes this to the \mathrm {FFN} i.e. \mathbf {y} = \mathbf {x} + \mathrm {FFN}(\mathrm {LN}(\mathbf {x} + \mathrm {MHA}(\mathrm {LN}(\mathbf {x})))). However, this requires waiting for the \mathrm {MHA} to complete before performing the \mathrm {FFN} computation. It has been found that performing these in parallel does not cause any severe degradation in performance. Explicitly, instead they do \mathbf {y} = \mathbf {x} + \mathrm {FFN}(\mathrm {LN}(\mathbf {x})) + \mathrm {MHA}(\mathrm {LN}(\mathbf {x}))

fr:title.text: What is the role of weight decay in training large models?

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: What is the role of weight decay in training large models?

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Since there is so much data, it is not feasible to train your model for multiple epochs. This is actually nice in the sense that we do not have to worry about overfitting and performing regularization.

However, many of the large models are still trained with weight decay. Tatsu claims that this is not to do with regularization but actually due to some weird interaction with the learning rate schedule. I am not sure I entirely understood this part.

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Notation. For this section, our notion of an embedding is a function f that takes a token and a position i.e. f(x, i) is the embedding of token x, occurring at position i.

💡 Insight. The inner product of two embeddings should only need relative positioning i.e. for any two \mathbf {x}, \mathbf {y} with positions i and j respectively, there should exist some g, such that \langle f(\mathbf {x}, i), f(\mathbf {y}, j) \rangle = g(\mathbf {x}, \mathbf {y}, i - j) Namely, this can be written as a function of just the relative positioning between the embeddings.
fr:mainmatter.html:p: The simplest transformation that only preserves this relative information are rotations.

html:figure.html:figcaption: Rotating embeddings does not change relative position.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: However, rotations are only easily defined for \mathbb {R}^2. To get around this, they simply partition their input 2d slices and apply the rotation via \mathrm {ROT} = \begin {bmatrix} \cos m \theta _{1} & - \sin m \theta _{1} & & & \\ \sin m \theta _{1} & \cos m \theta _{1} & & & \\ & & \cos m \theta _{2} & -\sin m \theta _{2} & \\ & & \sin m \theta _{2} & \cos m \theta _{2} & \\ & & & & \ddots \end {bmatrix}
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. The \theta _i's are fixed and chosen according to some schedule to capture different "frequencies".
fr:mainmatter.html:p: The embedding are used by appling them to the query and key matrices separately i.e. W_K = \mathrm {ROT}(W_K), \quad W_Q = \mathrm {ROT}(W_Q) These are then what is used in self-attention.
A lot of deep learning is empirical magic. A quick look behind the curtain into this magic awaits below...

html:ul.html:li: d_{\text {model}} is the input dimension
d_{\text {ff}} is the hidden dimension

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Exception 1. in the case of GLUs, we recall that to account for extra parameters we scale \frac {2}{3}. Therefore, d_{\text {ff}} = \frac {8}{3}d_{\text {model}}.
Exception 2. In T5, they use d_{\text {ff}} = 64d_{\text {model}}. The logic for this was that we could maximize the MFU by increasing matrix size. Ended up going back to small d_{\text {model}}.

html:ul.html:li: I believe the d_{\text {head}} is the output dim of each head.
This just says the total output dim for n heads is the same as if we did one head.
Means there is something important about splitting up the heads.

fr:mainmatter.html:p:
Rule 3. Aspect ratio = \frac {d_{\text {model}}}{n_{\text {layer}}} \approx 100-200
Pipeline dependent: if network speed is fast than parallelizing is easier and shallow networks are better.
If poor network speed then pipeline parallel might be more viable and deeper networks would be more parallelizable.

Rule 4. Vocab size
For single language models, vocab size is typically 30-50k
For multi language models, vocab size is typically 100-250k

The softmax is ill-behaved due to the exponentials. In the transformer, we have two softmaxes: one at the end and one in the attention.
For the first, note \log \sigma (\mathbf {x})_i = \log (x_i) - \log \underbrace {\sum _{j=1}^{d} e^{x_j}}_{D(\mathbf {x})} The only problem is the denominator term. The idea is to enforce the D(\mathbf {x}) = 1 by regularizing via a penalty on \log D(\mathbf {x}) ie. \mathcal {L}_{\text {aux}} = 10^{-4} \log ^2(D(\mathbf {x})) With the \nabla D(\mathbf {x}) should be 0 and we are effectively only considering the non-exponential term.
For the attention, they primarily operate on the logits prior to the softmax. One way is via layer norm before the softmax. Another is via 39ZN
Notes on the paper Fast Transformer Decoding: One Write-Head is All You Need.

For,
b batch dimension,
n sequence length,
d hidden dimension,
h number of heads. The total FLOPS for attention are roughly bnd^2. This comes from
3nd^2 FLOPS to compute Q = XW_Q, K = XW_K, V = XW_V.
Need to do this b times for each input in batch. and the total memory accesses are roughly \underbrace {bnd}_{X} + \underbrace {bhn^2}_{\text {softmax}} + \underbrace {d^2}_{\text {projection}}. This gives high arithmetic intensity O\left (\left (\frac {h}{d} + \frac {1}{bn}\right )^{-1}\right )

However, if we do incremental inference then we must multiply our total number of memory accesses by n i.e. bn^2d + nd^2. This gives arithmetic intensity O\left (\left (\frac {n}{d} + \frac {1}{b}\right )^{-1}\right ), which requires large batch and short sequence length.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. I believe we just ignore the softmax memory contribution in the inference case because it does not scale with n anymore as we are computing the logits just for the next token. Therefore, it becomes a \frac {d}{h} term, which we can safely ignore?
fr:mainmatter.html:p: The key is that the \frac {n}{d} term comes from the bn^2d term, where we are loading h (b \times n \times d / h) K and V matrices n times. Thus, we can improve the \frac {n}{d} term by a factor of h by simply not using a different K and V matrix for each head. This is the entire idea behind MQA.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. Similar to the KV-cache, we still can use h Q matrices because we do not need to load n of them into memory because only the last one matters for inference.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Group query attention is the same idea as MQA but instead of using one K and V for every head, they use a group of them. Where obviously the number of groups needs to be less than the number of heads.

html:figure.html:figcaption: Grouped query uses subset of K and V matrices.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Also inspired by this idea of reducing the dependence of K and V on the sequence length is sliding window attention.

The idea behind sliding window attention is to ensure the number of weight matrices needed for the keys K and values V does not scale with the sequence length. Intuitively, this means allowing each word to "attend" to only some fixed number of previous words rather than the whole sequence

html:figure.html:figcaption: "the" only sees "on" and "sat" rather than the full sentence.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: I used LazyVim for a long time, but I kept hitting a wall: understanding how LazyVim did something was consistently harder than if I had just written the thing myself. So I gave myself one day to rebuild from scratch, with the modest goal of first-class support for \TeX , Python, Forester, and C/C++. That one-day sprint is long past, and the config has kept evolving since — this note describes where it landed, not the frantic first pass. The through-line is that almost everything now leans on Neovim's own primitives instead of a framework, which is exactly the transparency I was missing.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. The whole config lives in my dotfiles. It is a thin init.lua that loads four modules — config.lazy, config.options, config.keymaps, and core.lsp — with the actual plugin specs under lua/plugins/ and per-server LSP tables under lsp/.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: To my surprise, a lot of the behavior I had taken for granted turned out to be options LazyVim set internally. I was greeted on first launch by 8-space indents and a clipboard that would not survive a trip between terminal instances. So the first job was archaeology: I dug through LazyVim for every option I actually liked and copied them into config/options.lua. System clipboard, sensible folding via Treesitter (foldexpr = v:lua.vim.treesitter.foldexpr()), a global statusline, conceallevel for markup — the unglamorous foundation everything else sits on.

The plugin manager is lazy.nvim, the de facto standard, and it genuinely just works. The bootstrap snippet from their docs clones it on first run, and after that every file under lua/plugins/ that returns a spec table is imported automatically. No central manifest to keep in sync — a new file is a new plugin.

This is where the config diverged most sharply from where it started. The first pass used mason.nvim, mason-lspconfig, and nvim-lspconfig — the standard stack. All three are now gone. Neovim 0.11 ships a native LSP configuration API: vim.lsp.config defines a server and vim.lsp.enable turns it on, and any table returned from lsp/<name>.lua on the runtime path is picked up as that server's config. So core.lsp is just a list —

— and each server gets a plain Lua file in lsp/. No package manager, no bridge plugin translating between two config schemas. I install the servers themselves through my system package manager and let Neovim wire them up. It is dramatically less machinery for the same result, and when something breaks there is exactly one place to look.

The Python setup is the clearest upgrade. I dropped pyright for basedpyright and split responsibilities: basedpyright does type checking and inlay hints, while ruff runs as its own language server handling import organization and diagnostics. In the LazyVim days I fought constantly with import resolution, hand-writing a function to locate each project's virtualenv and pin pythonPath to its interpreter. basedpyright resolves environments well enough on its own that the hack is gone. clangd, texlab, and lua_ls round out the set, each configured in its own file — texlab in particular carries a fair amount of custom command wiring for building, forward search into Zathura, and environment manipulation.

A shared LspAttach autocommand in core.lsp binds the buffer-local LSP keymaps once, for every server: go-to-definition and friends route through fzf-lua pickers, and document highlighting, inlay-hint toggling, and native completion get enabled per-buffer based on what each client actually supports.

fr:title.text: Forester finally has a real language server

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Forester finally has a real language server

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The original post described an ugly hack to get Forester completion. The forester.nvim plugin exposed a completion source, but only for nvim-cmp; blink.cmp (which I use — see below) needs a blink.compat shim, and that shim expected a plugin to be a source rather than to bury one inside a larger feature set. Getting reference completion working meant reading blink.compat internals and registering the source by hand.

None of that survives, because Forester now ships an actual language server. My lsp/forester.lua just launches forester lsp and hands it the forest root. The one wrinkle worth recording: forester locates the forest by its process working directory and ignores the LSP rootUri, and Neovim does not spawn the server in root_dir by default. So the config resolves the nearest forest.toml and starts the RPC process with an explicit cwd — otherwise the server exits with status 1 whenever Neovim's cwd has no forest.toml (e.g. when I open a tree from $HOME). Reference completion — indispensable for writing this very blog — now flows through the standard LSP path with no shim in sight.

Completion is blink.cmp, the successor that finally displaced nvim-cmp. Its sources are lsp, path, snippets, and omni, with LuaSnip as the snippet engine. Because I lean on native LSP, the on_attach handler calls vim.lsp.completion.enable with autotrigger, so the server's completions feed the picker directly.

Layered on top is supermaven-nvim for AI ghost-text suggestions — accept-suggestion, accept-word, and clear bound to <C-l>, <C-j>, and <C-]>. It fills the role Copilot-style tools play elsewhere without getting in the way of the structured LSP and snippet completions.

Going all the way back to the Gilles Castel blog post, I have always wanted snippets that auto-expand. Under LazyVim I had settled for friendly-snippets on native Neovim snippets; rebuilding from scratch, I refused to compromise. The engine is LuaSnip, which blink.cmp supports natively, and I have to plug the luasnip-latex-snippets repo that ports the original UltiSnips math snippets over. My own snippets live in a small local plugin, custom-luasnip-snippets.nvim, vendored under lua/local/.

The trick I had wanted for years and can finally do: load LaTeX math snippets while inside math environments in Forester. The snippet plugin runs with use_treesitter = true, so it inspects the Forester Treesitter grammar, finds the node types for math environments, and only expands the math snippets when the cursor is actually inside one. Writing \TeX inside a .tree file feels exactly like writing it in a .tex file.

Formatting runs through conform.nvim on save. Python is handled by ruff_format followed by pyink, Lua by stylua — this replaced the old black + isort pairing, folding most of it into the same ruff that already runs as a language server. Formatting falls back to the LSP formatter when no dedicated tool is configured for a filetype.

The fuzzy finder is fzf-lua, which replaced Telescope. File and grep pickers are scoped to a detected project root — a small utils.root module (lifted from LazyVim) walks LSP workspace folders, .git, and cwd to find it — with a dedicated tex override that restricts live-grep to \TeX source files.

The piece I am most fond of is forester-new, a local plugin that wires the editor into the forest's own note pipeline. It shells out to scripts/new so every tree gets the standard frontmatter and routing, then commands like :ForesterNew, :ForesterStub, :ForesterCitek, and :ForesterTransient handle the follow-through: creating a tree and inserting a \transclude, stubbing a concept and dropping a [[id]] link at the cursor, or — in visual mode — lifting a selection into a new tree and replacing it in place with the transclude. Stub-and-link without ever leaving the sentence, which is exactly the friction the forest workflow is supposed to remove.

I originally built a whole in-editor Anki workflow on top of AnkiConnect and a vendored anki.nvim. That has since moved out of the editor and into the forest itself: cards are just \taxon{Card} trees, and scripts/anki-sync mirrors them into Anki — creating the note on first sync and updating in place thereafter. Writing a card is now the same as writing any other tree, snippets and all, and the sync is one command away. This is an example of a card.

A compiler transformation that restructures a loop so the steady-state body contains instructions from multiple logical iterations, each at a different stage — statically constructing the overlap an OoO core would find dynamically. Canonical algorithm: modulo scheduling.
Two design philosophies for an instruction set architecture.

An ISA with many specialized instructions, including ones that combine memory access with arithmetic or operate on complex types (strings, decimal numbers). One instruction often does the work of several. x86 is the canonical example.
An ISA with a small set of simple, uniform instructions; explicit load and store; arithmetic only on registers. Each instruction does little but maps cleanly to hardware. PowerPC, MIPS, ARM are RISC.

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Problem. CISC needs a fat Microarchitecture engine to translate its complex instructions into hardware-executable primitives. That's silicon area, power, and design complexity that doesn't directly add throughput.

Solution. Once RISC clearly won as a hardware model, x86 vendors adopted instruction translation: a CISC front-end decodes x86 into RISC-like Micro-operations internally. The chip is RISC on the inside, CISC on the outside.

Remark. The cost of legacy is real. The original Pentium spent roughly 30% of its transistors on legacy support (mostly the microcode ROM). The Pentium Pro spent 40% on backward compatibility. Modern x86 chips have absorbed this overhead because they can — transistor budgets grew faster than the legacy tax.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: An ISA with a small set of simple, uniform instructions; explicit load and store; arithmetic only on registers. Each instruction does little but maps cleanly to hardware. PowerPC, MIPS, ARM are RISC.
The process abstraction, the syscalls for working with processes on Unix, the hardware-software contract that makes time-sharing safe, and the scheduling policies that decide who runs when.
A running program, plus all the state the OS needs to pause it and resume it later. A process consists of:

html:ol.html:li: Memory: an Address space holding the program's code, stack, and heap.
Registers: general-purpose registers carrying current computation, plus special-purpose registers:

html:ul.html:li: Program counter (PC): the next instruction to run.
Stack pointer and frame pointer: where the process sits on its stack.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A process moves between three states:

html:ul.html:li: running: currently on a CPU.
ready: runnable, waiting for the Scheduling to give it a CPU.
blocked: waiting on I/O or another event, not runnable.

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. Threads (see Concurrency Intro) share an address space within a process but each have their own PC, register set, and stack.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The minimum operations any OS must expose for working with Processes:

html:ol.html:li: Create: load a program from disk, set up its address space (stack, heap, code), and jump to main. The OS also initializes argc/argv.
Destroy: kill a misbehaving or unwanted process.
Wait: block until another process completes — useful for ordering dependent work.
Miscellaneous control: e.g. suspend / resume.
Status: report on running processes.

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. The stack is allocated up front. The heap starts small and grows via malloc, which calls into the OS (brk/sbrk) when it runs out of headroom.
fr:mainmatter.html:p: On Unix, the concrete realization of this API uses fork(), wait(), and exec() — covered separately.

The kernel-side data structure holding all per-process state. There is one PCB per Process, threaded together into the process list. The PCB is what the OS reads and writes during a Context switch: the saved register set lives here, as does the process state, the open files, the parent pointer, and so on.
An example from xv6:
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. Threads need an analogous thread control block; see Concurrency Intro.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The Unix system call that duplicates the calling Process. After fork() returns, two processes exist where there was one. The child gets its own copy of:

html:ol.html:li: Address space.
General-purpose registers.
Program counter — the child returns from fork() at the same instruction the parent does.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The two processes are distinguished by fork()'s return value:
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. Which process is scheduled first after fork() is non-deterministic. Use wait() if you need ordering.
Remark. The copy is logical, not physical: real implementations use Copy-on-write so the duplicated address space only allocates fresh pages when one side writes to them.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Blocks the calling Process until one of its children terminates. The OS marks the child's PCB ZOMBIE on exit, keeping enough state (exit status, resource usage) for the parent to retrieve via wait(); only when the parent collects it is the child fully reaped.
wait() returns the PID of the child that died. Variant waitpid(pid, ...) blocks on a specific child — useful when a parent has multiple children and only one is interesting.
Used to enforce ordering between parent and child code paths after fork(), or to know when an exec()'d program finishes.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. Calling wait() with no children to wait on returns -1.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Replaces the calling Process's program with a new executable. exec() does not return on success — the original code, data, stack, and heap are discarded and the new program runs in their place. The PID is unchanged.
What exec() does internally:

html:ol.html:li: Loads the new executable's code and static data, overwriting the current.
Re-initializes stack and heap.
Jumps to the new program's entry point with arguments passed as argv.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The canonical Unix pattern is fork() then exec() — the shell uses this to launch a command without becoming that command:
Redirection is implemented by opening a file in the child between fork() and exec(), attached to STDOUT_FILENO:
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. The many variants — execl, execle, execlp, execv, execvp, execvP — differ in how they take arguments (list vs. vector), whether they use the PATH environment variable, and whether they take an explicit environment.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: An asynchronous notification delivered to a Process. A process sends a signal with kill(pid, signum) (the name is misleading — most signals don't kill); the recipient can install a handler with signal(signum, handler) to "catch" it, otherwise the default action runs (often termination).

Signals are the OS's mechanism for events the recipient didn't ask to be told about: SIGINT from Ctrl-C, SIGSEGV from a Page fault on an invalid page, SIGCHLD when a child exits, SIGTERM from an administrative kill.
Who can signal whom depends on the user owning the process — a user can signal their own processes; the superuser can signal any process.

The OS lets user code run directly on the CPU for speed, but with hardware-enforced limits that prevent a buggy or malicious program from compromising the rest of the system. Two questions motivate the mechanism:

html:li.html:em: How do we make sure a program doesn't do what we don't want it to do?

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The first question is answered by hardware User mode vs kernel mode enforced via the Trap table, with a controlled Syscall mechanism as the only path from one to the other. The second is answered by periodic timer interrupts that trap to the OS, which performs a Context switch and runs the scheduler.
The whole stack is bootstrapped during the Boot sequence, when the kernel installs the trap table and starts the timer before handing control to user space.

Two privilege levels supported by the CPU. User mode restricts the running process to a limited set of instructions and a limited view of memory — usually just its own address space. Kernel mode runs the OS with full access to all instructions and all of physical memory.

Switching between modes is mediated by hardware via a Trap table populated at boot. The OS sets up the table; the CPU enforces it. A process cannot promote itself to kernel mode arbitrarily — it must invoke a Syscall mechanism, which traps to a fixed entry point in the OS.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. Kernel mode is dangerous because the address space is effectively the entire machine. The mode bit + the trap table together are what makes it safe to time-share a CPU between mutually distrusting programs.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A hardware-readable table installed by the OS at boot that maps each kind of exceptional control-flow event (syscall, interrupt, page fault, illegal instruction, ...) to the address of an OS handler. When the CPU takes a trap, it consults this table to decide where to jump, switches into kernel mode, and runs the handler.

The table is what makes it safe to expose privileged operations to user mode: user code never gets to pick the destination of a mode-switch, only the trap number. The OS controls every entry point, the CPU enforces the indirection.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. Installing the trap table is itself a privileged operation. It happens once, early in boot, before any user process exists.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A syscall is a user-mode-initiated request for a privileged service from the kernel. Because user code cannot jump into kernel code directly, the mechanism is built around a single trap instruction plus a pre-agreed register convention identifying which service to perform.
The end-to-end sequence:

html:ol.html:li: User code places the syscall number in a pre-defined register and invokes the trap instruction.
Hardware saves enough state to resume the process and switches to kernel mode.
Hardware jumps to the OS-installed handler via the Trap table.
The handler reads the syscall number from the agreed register and dispatches to the corresponding OS routine.
The OS validates the request, performs the work, and arranges return values per the calling convention.
The OS restores user state and returns the process to user mode.

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. Because the register convention is fixed by ABI, the user-mode side of a syscall is typically hand-written in assembly within libc.
Remark. Interrupts work via a similar trap-handler mechanism, but are initiated by hardware (timer, device) rather than user code.
Remark. The kernel must validate any pointers or arguments the user supplies — user code cannot be trusted not to ask the kernel to read kernel memory on its behalf.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The operation of saving the full execution state of one process (or thread) and restoring another's so the CPU can resume it as if it had never been interrupted. On a trap into the kernel, the hardware stashes the user-visible state (PC, condition codes, stack pointer) into a known location, and the OS saves the remaining general-purpose registers as part of the switching code.
Two flavors of state are involved:

html:ol.html:li: User registers: the current program's context (general-purpose registers, stack vars).
Kernel registers: meta-information used to resume the trap correctly (PC at trap, syscall number, etc.).

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Context switches are not free — they incur the cost of cache and TLB pollution in addition to the direct cost of saving/restoring state. This is the cost amortized by larger scheduling quanta and a recurring concern for any Concurrency system.
The fixed series of steps a machine performs from power-on until a usable operating system is running. Each stage runs from a known location and hands off control to the next.

html:ol.html:li: Power signal resets the CPU to a known state.
The CPU fetches from a hard-wired address — the BIOS (or UEFI firmware). The BIOS runs basic hardware checks (POST).
The BIOS loads and jumps to the bootloader (e.g. GRUB) from a known location on disk.
The bootloader places the kernel image at the correct address and jumps to its entry point.
The kernel performs early setup: installs the Trap table (a special privileged instruction), starts the timer interrupt, sets up memory management, and finally enters user space.

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. The hardware is hard-wired only to find the BIOS. Everything past that is software the user can replace, which is why dual-booting and custom bootloaders work.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The scheduler decides which runnable Process to put on the CPU at each moment. There is no single right answer because the metrics conflict:

html:ol.html:li: Turnaround time: time from arrival to completion, T_{\text {completion}} - T_{\text {arrival}}. Minimized by running short jobs first.
Response time: time from arrival to first scheduling. Minimized by frequent rotation.
Fairness: every job makes progress; nobody starves.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: First-in, first-out. Jobs are run to completion in the order they arrive. Trivially fair under any reasonable notion of fairness, but suffers from the convoy effect: a single long-running job at the head delays every shorter job behind it, inflating average turnaround time.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Problem. If the first arriving job is much longer than the rest, all subsequent jobs wait at least its full duration before getting any CPU.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Shortest job first. Run the job with the smallest total work first, run-to-completion. If all jobs arrive at the same time, SJF provably minimizes average turnaround time.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Problem. Real workloads don't all arrive at t = 0. A long job that arrives slightly before a short one still gets to monopolize the CPU until it finishes — SJF degenerates to FIFO scheduling under sequential arrivals.
fr:mainmatter.html:p: Removing the run-to-completion constraint and allowing preemption gives STCF scheduling.

Shortest time-to-completion first. A preemptive refinement of SJF scheduling: whenever the scheduler is invoked, it picks the job with the least remaining work. A short job arriving mid-execution can preempt a long one.
STCF is provably optimal for minimizing average turnaround time over all preemptive schedulers.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Problem. Long jobs starve in response time. The lowest-priority work might not get a CPU slice for a very long time, which is unacceptable for interactive workloads.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Cycle through all runnable jobs, giving each a fixed time slice — the stride, time slice, or quantum length — before preempting and moving to the next. The best you can do at minimizing response time: every job sees the CPU within at most N quanta where N is the runnable-job count.
The quantum length is a tradeoff:

html:ul.html:li: Short quantum: better response time, more frequent context switches, worse cache locality.
Long quantum: amortizes context-switch cost, worse response time.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Worst-case response time for N jobs at quantum S is S(N-1).
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. Round-robin minimizes response time but is poor for turnaround time relative to STCF scheduling. A scheduler that wants both has to combine ideas — see Multi-level feedback queue.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A practical scheduler that approximates STCF scheduling's turnaround properties and Round-robin scheduling's response properties without requiring foreknowledge of job lengths. Maintain multiple priority queues; within a queue, schedule round-robin. Never service a lower-priority queue while a higher-priority queue has a runnable job.
The priority of a job is not assigned by the user — it is learned from behavior:

html:ul.html:li: New jobs enter at the top priority.
Each priority level has an allotment — the total CPU time a job may consume before being demoted.
Once a job exhausts its allotment at a level, it drops to the next level.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Short jobs that finish quickly never leave the top queue (good turnaround). Interactive jobs that yield often stay at the top (good response time). CPU-bound jobs sink to the bottom and get scheduled when nothing better is runnable.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Problem. Gaming the allotment: a job can issue I/O just before its allotment expires to avoid demotion, since I/O time doesn't count.
Solution. Charge the job's allotment for total time at the priority level, including time spent blocked.
Problem. Starvation of low-priority queues if higher queues never empty.
Solution. Periodically reset all jobs to the top priority. Cheap and effective.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A proportional-share scheduler. Each runnable job holds some number of tickets, drawn from a fixed total. On each scheduling decision, the scheduler picks a random ticket and runs the job that owns it. Over time, a job receives CPU time proportional to its share of the tickets.
The implementation is trivial: one random number per decision, plus a sum table. No global state about historical CPU usage is needed.
Mechanisms layered on top:

html:ul.html:li: Ticket currency — give each user a fixed number of tickets to split among their processes however they like.
Ticket transfer — a client process can hand tickets to a server to boost its priority while servicing the request.
Ticket inflation — a process can grow its share temporarily, with bounds set by trust.

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. The unsolved problem is how to assign tickets in the first place. The mechanism is simple; the policy is not.
fr:mainmatter.html:p: A deterministic version of the same idea is Stride scheduling, which avoids the variance of random sampling at the cost of needing global state.

A deterministic proportional-share scheduler. Each job has a stride inversely proportional to its ticket count (the more tickets, the smaller the stride) and a running pass counter that accumulates its stride every time it's scheduled.
The decision is:

html:ol.html:li: Pick the job with the lowest pass.
Run it for one quantum.
Increment its pass by its stride.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A job with twice as many tickets has half the stride, so its pass grows half as fast, so it gets scheduled twice as often. Same long-run behavior as Lottery scheduling, but with zero variance.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Problem. New jobs need an initial pass value. Setting it to 0 lets them dominate; setting it to the global minimum lets them merge in cleanly but requires tracking that minimum.
Remark. Each context switch is not free. Choosing a longer time slice amortizes that cost at the price of response time.
Remark. I/O-bound jobs are commonly handled by splitting a job into its non-I/O segments and scheduling them individually — turning one long-but-mostly-blocked job into many short, runnable ones.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Deliberate-Practice (1/66)
The process abstraction, what the OS has to support to maintain it, and the data structure that holds it together.
A running program, plus all the state the OS needs to pause it and resume it later. A process consists of:

html:ol.html:li: Memory: an Address space holding the program's code, stack, and heap.
Registers: general-purpose registers carrying current computation, plus special-purpose registers:

html:ul.html:li: Program counter (PC): the next instruction to run.
Stack pointer and frame pointer: where the process sits on its stack.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A process moves between three states:

html:ul.html:li: running: currently on a CPU.
ready: runnable, waiting for the Scheduling to give it a CPU.
blocked: waiting on I/O or another event, not runnable.

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. Threads (see Concurrency Intro) share an address space within a process but each have their own PC, register set, and stack.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The minimum operations any OS must expose for working with Processes:

html:ol.html:li: Create: load a program from disk, set up its address space (stack, heap, code), and jump to main. The OS also initializes argc/argv.
Destroy: kill a misbehaving or unwanted process.
Wait: block until another process completes — useful for ordering dependent work.
Miscellaneous control: e.g. suspend / resume.
Status: report on running processes.

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. The stack is allocated up front. The heap starts small and grows via malloc, which calls into the OS (brk/sbrk) when it runs out of headroom.
fr:mainmatter.html:p: On Unix, the concrete realization of this API uses fork(), wait(), and exec() — covered separately.

The kernel-side data structure holding all per-process state. There is one PCB per Process, threaded together into the process list. The PCB is what the OS reads and writes during a Context switch: the saved register set lives here, as does the process state, the open files, the parent pointer, and so on.
An example from xv6:
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. Threads need an analogous thread control block; see Concurrency Intro.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Worked exercises for this chapter are parked separately in Three Easy Pieces: Exercises under the Processes Intro section.

An independent unit of execution within a Process. Each thread has its own program counter, register set, and stack — but shares the surrounding address space, file descriptors, and other process-level resources with its siblings.

To support a context switch between threads, the OS keeps a thread control block (TCB) per thread — the per-thread analog of Process control block. Each thread also gets its own stack within the process's address space.
Why threads instead of processes:

html:ol.html:li: Intra-process parallelism: parallelizable work within one program, without paying the context switch cost of separate processes.
Avoiding blocking I/O: one thread blocks waiting on I/O while others keep running.

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. Sharing an address space is exactly what makes threads cheap to communicate between and exactly what makes them dangerous — see Race condition and Critical section.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Worked homework for Three Easy Pieces. One section per chapter; the conceptual material for each chapter lives in the corresponding concept pages — Processes Intro across Process, Process API, Process control block; Process Interlude across fork(), wait(), exec(), Unix signals.

html:ol.html:li: For what types of workloads does SJF scheduling deliver the same turnaround as FIFO scheduling?
html:ol.html:div: Answer. For workloads that arrive in increasing order of total job time.
html:ol.html:li: For what types of workloads and quantum lengths does SJF deliver the same response times as Round-robin scheduling?
html:ol.html:div: Answer. For shorter jobs and longer quantum lengths, RR and SJF should deliver similar response times.
html:ol.html:li: What happens to response time with SJF as job lengths increase?
html:ol.html:div: Answer. Response time degrades because the longest jobs have to wait for each of the shorter jobs to finish. If these shorter jobs become longer than the response time grows linearly.
html:ol.html:li: What happens to response time with RR as quantum lengths increase? Can you write an equation that gives the worst-case response time, given N jobs?
html:ol.html:div: Answer. The response time is the average over stride length number of preceding jobs. The formula can be written as \mathrm {RT} = \frac {1}{N} \sum _{i = 1}^{N} (i - 1)S = \frac {S(N - 1) N}{N} = S(N - 1)
html:ol.html:li:
Run process.py with the flags -l 5:100, 5:100. What should the CPU utilization be?
Answer. The flags correspond to running two processes each with 5 instructions where each instruction has probability one of using the CPU. The output from the program is Process 0 cpu cpu cpu cpu cpu
Process 1 cpu cpu cpu cpu cpu with this, we can conclude that the utilization is 100%. We will execute one instruction for the first process until it completes, then the five instructions of the next process. If both are waiting on IO, then I do not think this would be the case?
Solution. Using the solution flags -cp, there is 100% utilization.

Run process.py with the flags -l 4:100, 1:0. How long does it take to complete both processes?
Answer. The flags correspond to running one process with 4 instructions with each instruction having probability one of using the CPU and one process with one instruction that uses the IO. The output from the program is Process 0 cpu cpu cpu cpu
Process 1 io io_done It will take 4 + \text {time of IO}. Since the process with 4 non-IO-dependent instructions will run each of those instructions and then the next process will begin which must wait for the entire duration of IO.
Solution. Using the -cp flags it takes 11 timesteps to complete which is \approx 4 + \text {time of IO}.

Now switch the order of the processes: ./process-run.py -l 1:0,4:100. What happens now? Does switching the order mat- ter? Why?
Answer. The output of the code is Process 0 io io_done
Process 1 cpu cpu cpu cpu Depending on the time of IO (the -L flag), it should take \max (5, 1 + \text {time IO}). Switching the order does matter because we can run the CPU instructions while waiting for non-blocking IO. This motivates the idea of scheduling.
Solution. It only takes 7 timesteps because we are able to perform the IO operations off the CPU as expected.

We'll now explore some of the other flags. One important flag is -S, which determines how the system reacts when a process issues an I/O. With the flag set to SWITCH_ON_END, the system will NOT switch to another process while one is doing I/O, instead waiting until the process is completely finished. What happens when you run the following two processes, one doing I/O and the other doing CPU work? (-l 1:0,4:100 -c -S SWITCH_ON_END)
Answer. The output of the code is: Time PID: 0 PID: 1 CPU IOs 1 RUN:io READY 1 2 BLOCKED READY 1 3 BLOCKED READY 1 4 BLOCKED READY 1 5 BLOCKED READY 1 6 BLOCKED READY 1 7* RUN:io_done READY 1 8 DONE RUN:cpu 1 9 DONE RUN:cpu 1 10 DONE RUN:cpu 1 11 DONE RUN:cpu 1 As expected, it runs the IO process, blocking the CPU process until it is completed.

Now, run the same processes, but with the switching behavior set to switch to another process whenever one is WAITING for I/O (-l 1:0,4:100 -c -S SWITCH ON IO). What happens now? Use -c and -p to confirm that you are right
html:ol.html:div: Answer. I assume that at timestep 1 we will still RUN:io. However, we will then switch the PID: 1 for the next 4 timesteps until it is completed. After one additional BLOCKED timestep, we will run RUN:io_done and complete both processes.

Solution. The output of the code is: Time PID: 0 PID: 1 CPU IOs 1 RUN:io READY 1 2 BLOCKED RUN:cpu 1 1 3 BLOCKED RUN:cpu 1 1 4 BLOCKED RUN:cpu 1 1 5 BLOCKED RUN:cpu 1 1 6 BLOCKED DONE 1 7* RUN:io_done DONE 1
html:ol.html:li:
One other important behavior is what to do when an I/O completes. With -I IO RUN LATER, when an I/O completes, the process that issued it is not necessarily run right away; rather, whatever was running at the time keeps running. What happens when you run this combination of processes? (./process-run.py -l 3:0,5:100,5:100,5:100 -S SWITCH ON IO -I IO RUN LATER -c -p) Are system resources being effectively utilized?
Answer. The resources are not efficiently utilized because we end up waiting until all of our CPU-bound processes finish before returning to the IO-bound process, which calls another IO:run and we do not get any concurrency benefit.
Solution. The code output is pretty long but the end output is Stats: Total Time 31 Stats: CPU Busy 21 (67.74%) Stats: IO Busy 15 (48.39%)

Now run the same processes, but with -I IO RUN IMMEDIATE set, which immediately runs the process that issued the I/O. How does this behavior differ? Why might running a process that just completed an I/O again be a good idea?
Answer. The behavior differs by returning to the IO-bound process, which can then immediately call the next IO:run before returning access to the CPU-bound processes, improving concurrency. In an attempt to predict the next IO call, it might be a good idea to run the process that just completed IO because it is more likely to make another IO call.
Solution. The corresponding statistics are Stats: Total Time 21 Stats: CPU Busy 21 (100.00%) Stats: IO Busy 15 (71.43%)

Now run with some randomly generated processes, e.g., -s 1 -l 3:50,3:50, -s 2 -l 3:50,3:50, -s 3 -l 3:50,3:50. See if you can predict how the trace will turn out. What happens when you use -I IO RUN IMMEDIATE vs. -I IO RUN LATER? What hap- pens when you use -S SWITCH ON IO vs. -S SWITCH ON END?
Answer. Since each instruction has a 50% chance of being CPU or I/O, I do not think there will be much benefit to IO_RUN_LATER vs. IO_RUN_IMMEDIATE. However, I believe it will be crucial to enable -S SWITCH_ON_IO over -S SWITCH_ON_END as the number of processes grow because we should always have waiting CPU instructions to run. In the case of two processes, we still may not improve that much because they will likely both be IO-bound at some point.
Solution. For -s 1 (aka the seed), the observed performance between IO_RUN_IMMEDIATE and IO_RUN_LATER is the same. However, we do observe an improvement on both IO and CPU usage when we enable SWITCH_ON_IO.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Deliberate-Practice (6/66).

html:ol.html:li: Write a program that calls fork(). Before calling fork(), have the main process access a variable (e.g., x) and set its value to something (e.g., 100). What value is the variable in the child process? What happens to the variable when both the child and parent change the value of x
html:ol.html:div: Answer. Both the parent and the child get a copy of the variable values i.e. x. When they modify x it only affects the copy of x in their local address space.
html:ol.html:li: Write a program that opens a file (with the open() system call) and then calls fork() to create a new process. Can both the child and parent access the file descriptor returned by open()? What happens when they are writing to the file concurrently, i.e., at the same time?
html:ol.html:div: Answer. Both do have access to the same file descriptor. I believe that they can interleave writes if writing at the same time but for me whichever process got ran first always finished writing prior the other starting. This may be a scheduling thing, where one process is able to complete before the other is scheduled?
html:ol.html:li: Write another program using fork(). The child process should print "hello"; the parent process should print "goodbye". You should try to ensure that the child process always prints first; can you do this without calling wait() in the parent?
html:ol.html:div: Answer. Using the previous problem, in the child, we can write something to a file. Then, in the parent, we can enforce an ordering by making the parent wait in a while loop until it reads the value written by the child. However, this is terribly inconvenient (and probably why wait() exists).
html:ol.html:li: Now write a program that uses wait() to wait for the child process to finish in the parent. What does wait() return? What happens if you use wait() in the child?
html:ol.html:div: Answer. wait() returns the pid of the child process that died. Using wait() in the child just immediately returns -1.
html:ol.html:li: Write a program that calls fork() and then calls some form of exec() to run the program /bin/ls. See if you can try all of the variants of exec(), including execl(), execle(), execlp(), execv(), execvp(), and execvP(). Why do you think there are so many variants of the same basic call?
html:ol.html:div: Answer. It seems that some of them support different types of arguments i.e. execv family allows variable size argument list. Additionally, there is a distinction in the environment. This seems to be a list of environment variable values that exec{l,v}e() will search over. The p family use the same search as the shell i.e. the PATH.
html:ol.html:li: Write a slight modification of the previous program, this time using waitpid() instead of wait(). When would waitpid() be useful?
html:ol.html:div: Answer. If a parent process has multiple children, then wait() will only wait until the first one dies. waitpid() lets you specify waiting on a specific child process.
html:ol.html:li: Write a program that creates a child process, and then in the child closes standard output (STDOUT_FILENO). What happens if the child calls printf() to print some output after closing the descriptor?
html:ol.html:div: Answer. Nothing prints in the child process. However, stdout is still open in the parent process and able to print.
html:ol.html:li: Write a program that creates two children, and connects the standard output of one to the standard input of the other, using the pipe() system call
html:ol.html:div: Answer. This required fflush() for me. I am not sure if this is true in general?

fr:mainmatter.html:p: An independent unit of execution within a Process. Each thread has its own program counter, register set, and stack — but shares the surrounding address space, file descriptors, and other process-level resources with its siblings.

To support a context switch between threads, the OS keeps a thread control block (TCB) per thread — the per-thread analog of Process control block. Each thread also gets its own stack within the process's address space.
Why threads instead of processes:

html:ol.html:li: Intra-process parallelism: parallelizable work within one program, without paying the context switch cost of separate processes.
Avoiding blocking I/O: one thread blocks waiting on I/O while others keep running.

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. Sharing an address space is exactly what makes threads cheap to communicate between and exactly what makes them dangerous — see Race condition and Critical section.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A running program, plus all the state the OS needs to pause it and resume it later. A process consists of:

html:ol.html:li: Memory: an Address space holding the program's code, stack, and heap.
Registers: general-purpose registers carrying current computation, plus special-purpose registers:

html:ul.html:li: Program counter (PC): the next instruction to run.
Stack pointer and frame pointer: where the process sits on its stack.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A process moves between three states:

html:ul.html:li: running: currently on a CPU.
ready: runnable, waiting for the Scheduling to give it a CPU.
blocked: waiting on I/O or another event, not runnable.

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. Threads (see Concurrency Intro) share an address space within a process but each have their own PC, register set, and stack.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The operation of saving the full execution state of one process (or thread) and restoring another's so the CPU can resume it as if it had never been interrupted. On a trap into the kernel, the hardware stashes the user-visible state (PC, condition codes, stack pointer) into a known location, and the OS saves the remaining general-purpose registers as part of the switching code.
Two flavors of state are involved:

html:ol.html:li: User registers: the current program's context (general-purpose registers, stack vars).
Kernel registers: meta-information used to resume the trap correctly (PC at trap, syscall number, etc.).

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Context switches are not free — they incur the cost of cache and TLB pollution in addition to the direct cost of saving/restoring state. This is the cost amortized by larger scheduling quanta and a recurring concern for any Concurrency system.

Concurrency comes from sharing: multiple Threads within a single process share the address space, which is what makes them cheap to spawn and to communicate between, and is also what makes them dangerous. When threads interleave on a shared variable, you get a Race condition. The fragile region is the Critical section, protected by synchronization primitives like Locks and Barriers.

An independent unit of execution within a Process. Each thread has its own program counter, register set, and stack — but shares the surrounding address space, file descriptors, and other process-level resources with its siblings.

To support a context switch between threads, the OS keeps a thread control block (TCB) per thread — the per-thread analog of Process control block. Each thread also gets its own stack within the process's address space.
Why threads instead of processes:

html:ol.html:li: Intra-process parallelism: parallelizable work within one program, without paying the context switch cost of separate processes.
Avoiding blocking I/O: one thread blocks waiting on I/O while others keep running.

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. Sharing an address space is exactly what makes threads cheap to communicate between and exactly what makes them dangerous — see Race condition and Critical section.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: When the correctness of a program depends on the order in which concurrent Threads schedule, you have a race condition. The classic example: two threads each execute
fr:mainmatter.html:pre: mov addr reg // load add 1 reg // increment mov reg addr // store
fr:mainmatter.html:p: intending to increment a shared variable twice. If the OS interleaves the threads at the wrong instruction boundary:

html:ol.html:li: Thread 1 stores its incremented value.
Thread 2 stores its incremented value.
Thread 1 increments (in its register).
Thread 2 increments (in its register).

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The result is a single increment, not two — both threads loaded the same starting value and the second store clobbered the first.
The fix is to prevent interleaving inside the code region that touches shared state — the Critical section — using a synchronization primitive like Locks.

A region of code that accesses shared state in a way that is incorrect if more than one Thread executes it concurrently. Critical sections are exactly the regions that need protection by a synchronization primitive: a Locks excludes other threads while one is inside; a Barriers forces all threads to reach the same point before any may continue.
The art of writing correct concurrent code is to identify critical sections and protect them — without making them so large that you lose all parallelism.
